Good. Here's what was fixed and why:

---

### Root causes & fixes

| Problem | Fix |
|---|---|
| **Port still busy at startup** — previous uvicorn didn't release port before new spawn | `freePort()` checks the port, kills whoever holds it via `lsof -ti tcp:8000 \| xargs kill -9`, then waits up to 2 s for the OS to confirm it's free |
| **Electron crash / force-quit** — `before-quit` never fires, uvicorn stays as orphan | `freePort()` runs on every app start, so the orphan is killed regardless of how the previous instance died |
| **SIGTERM too slow** — uvicorn was still shutting down when next start hit the port | SIGKILL fallback fires after 2 s if the process doesn't exit on SIGTERM |
| **Double-call** — `window-all-closed` + `before-quit` both call `stopBackend()` on macOS Cmd+Q | `_stopCalled` guard ensures only the first call acts; the second is a no-op |

The key change is that **`freePort()` now runs before every `startBackend()`** — so no matter what happened to the previous run (clean exit, crash, manual start, whatever), the port is guaranteed free before uvicorn tries to bind it.

output:
**aicli** is a shared AI memory platform for developers.

The core problem it solves: when you use different AI tools (Claude CLI, Cursor, ChatGPT in the browser, etc.), each one starts from scratch — no memory of your project, your decisions, or your history.

**aicli gives all of them the same memory.**

Here's how:
- Every prompt you send and every AI response gets saved to a unified history
- Git commits get tagged to the feature or bug they belong to
- A "memory file" (CLAUDE.md / CONTEXT.md) is auto-generated and injected into every AI tool at session start — so Claude CLI, Cursor, and the web UI all know: _what is this project, what was decided last week, what feature are we working on_

**The UI (what you're looking at right now) adds:**
- A chat interface to multiple LLMs (Claude, GPT, Gemini, DeepSeek)
- A **Planner** — tag/feature/bug tracker where every AI conversation and commit is linked to a feature
- **History** — see every session (CLI or UI) grouped with the commits that happened during it
- **Workflows** — chain multiple AI agents together in a graph (design → review → implement → test)

**In one sentence:** it's a project-aware AI workspace where your entire development history — prompts, commits, decisions — is shared across every AI tool you use, so you never re-explain your project again.

BEFORE:

Now I have everything I need. Let me write the comprehensive comparison and design proposal.

---

## Workflow System Design: Comparison & Proposal

### What the two external systems do

**specrails** is essentially a **Claude Code agent system** — 12 specialized prompt files (`.md` role definitions) + slash commands (`/sr:implement`) that chain them. The "reviewer loop" is handled by Claude Code's native multi-turn. No visual editor, no programmatic DAG. Agent memory is stored in `.claude/agent-memory/` files. Very tightly coupled to Claude Code specifically.

**paperclip** is an **org chart orchestrator** — company hierarchy, heartbeat-based scheduling, budget enforcement, role titles with manager/subordinate reporting. It's for running autonomous agent teams long-term, not for composing development pipelines. A different category entirely.

---

### What aicli already has (and it's better than both)

Reading the existing code, aicli's `graph_runner.py` + `graph_workflow.js` already implements:

| Feature | specrails | paperclip | **aicli (existing)** |
|---------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Visual graph editor | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ Cytoscape.js |
| Multi-provider (Claude/OpenAI/DeepSeek/Gemini/Grok) | ✗ Claude only | ✓ any | ✓ all 5 |
| Reviewer loop (conditional back-edge) | ✓ slash cmd | ✗ | ✓ edge conditions |
| User approval gate | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ `require_approval` |
| Parallel node execution | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ `asyncio.gather` |
| Run history + cost tracking | ✗ | ✓ | ✓ DB |
| Memory-aware context | ✓ per-role files | ✗ | ✓ `project_facts` |
| YAML templates | ✗ | ✗ | ✓ 4 templates |

**The gap isn't the engine — it's the role library and user interaction.**

## aicli — Shared AI Memory Platform

**Core idea**: When you switch between different AI tools (Claude CLI, Cursor, ChatGPT, the aicli web UI), they all forget your project context. aicli solves this by giving every LLM the **same persistent memory**.

### What the code does

1. **5-Layer Memory System** — Stores context at 5 levels: live conversation → session state → project docs → full history (JSONL + PostgreSQL) → global templates. 5. **Electron + FastAPI UI** — An Electron desktop app with tabs for Chat, Planner, Prompts, Code, Workflows, History, and Settings. FastAPI backend (16 routers), vanilla JS frontend (no React/bundler).

6. **MCP Server** — 12+ tools exposed via Model Context Protocol (stdio) so Claude CLI and other MCP clients can query project state, search memory, create entities, sync GitHub issues, and run work-item pipelines.

7. **Billing & Auth** — Multi-user with JWT auth, 3 roles (admin/paid/free), per-request billing with markup and coupons.

## Claude Agent SDK vs Your Current Multi-Agent System

### What is the Claude Agent SDK?

The **Claude Agent SDK** is Anthropic's official Python/TypeScript framework for building AI agents that can:
- Execute tools (file read, bash, web search, custom MCP tools)
- Delegate to **subagents** (specialized Claude instances with different system prompts)
- Maintain conversation state across turns
- Handle streaming and user approvals

### Can it replace your PM → Developer → Tester → Reviewer pipeline?

**Short answer: No — your custom system is already more powerful for this use case.** Here's why:

| Capability | Claude Agent SDK | Your aicli DAG + Pipeline |
|---|---|---|
| **Loop-back** (Reviewer → Developer if score < 7) | Not native; workaround via resume | Built-in with iteration cap |
| **Multi-provider** (Haiku for PM, Claude for Dev, DeepSeek for review) | Claude only | 5 providers per node |
| **Conditional edges** | Via prompt instructions only | Native condition eval (eq, gte, contains...) |
| **Approval gates** | Limited (user input tool) | Native `require_approval` per node |
| **Parallel execution** | Subagents in parallel | `asyncio.gather()` on ready nodes |
| **Cost tracking per node** | Internal only | Full: tokens + cost per node + run totals |
| **Structured output** | Supported | Supported (JSON schema per node) |

## Architectural Review: 3-Layer Client/Project Tables

### What's Good

The three-tier naming is clear and the seeding pattern (`mng_` → `cl_local_` on first run) is clean. For a single-tenant tool it works well today.

---

### Key Concerns at Scale

**1. Table proliferation**

With the current design, each new client = 6 DDL statements, each new project = 10 more. At 20 clients × 10 projects each, you have 120 client tables + 2000 project tables in the `public` schema. PostgreSQL handles this technically, but:
- `pg_catalog` queries slow down
- Cross-client reporting requires dynamic SQL (`UNION ALL cl_acme_entity_values, cl_startup_entity_values, ...`)
- `\dt` output becomes unreadable

**2. The client name is baked into the table name**

If a client renames from `acme` to `acmecorp`, you'd have to `ALTER TABLE` 6 tables. That's a schema migration as a business operation — a bad coupling.

**3. No natural DB-level isolation**

All data is in `public` schema. A query bug touching `cl_acme_entity_values` instead of `cl_local_entity_values` silently reads the wrong client. There's no enforcement layer.

**4. Free/unregistered clients are implicit**

`client = "local"` is a magic string hardcoded in 9+ files and the `client_table()` default. There's no record of *what* a "local" client is, whether it has a plan, when it was created, etc.

---

### Architectural Options

| Approach | Isolation | Cross-client queries | Client count | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Current** (table-per-client) | Low | Hard (dynamic SQL) | ~20 max | Low |
| **Shared tables + `client_id` FK** | Medium (RLS) | Easy (WHERE clause) | Unlimited | Low |
| **PostgreSQL schema per client** | High (DROP SCHEMA) | Hard (SET search_path) | ~100 | Medium |
| **Separate database per client** | Full | Not possible | ~20 | High |

**For your goals (SaaS with multiple paying clients), I'd recommend Option B — shared tables with `client_id`.**

---

### Recommended Architecture

async def run_pipeline(project_id: str, session_id: str):
    # Layer 1 — session summary
    interactions = await db.fetch(
        "SELECT prompt, response FROM interactions 
         WHERE session_id = $1 ORDER BY created_at",
        session_id
    )
    summary = await llm.summarise(
        interactions,
        instruction="Extract key decisions, patterns, and outcomes. 
                     Be concise. Ignore small talk and failed attempts."
    )
    memory_id = await db.insert("memory_items", {
        "project_id": project_id,
         "scope": "session",
        "content": summary,
        "source_ids": [i.id for i in interactions],
        "tags": extract_tags(interactions)
    })
    # Layer 2 — extract facts from new memory
    facts = await llm.extract_facts(
        summary,
        instruction="Extract only durable architectural facts.
                     Return as JSON: [{key, value}]"
    )
    for fact in facts:
        # Invalidate old version if exists
        await db.execute(
            "UPDATE project_facts SET valid_until = now()
             WHERE project_id = $1 AND fact_key = $2 
             AND valid_until IS NULL",
            project_id, fact.key
        )
        await db.insert("project_facts", {
            "project_id": project_id,
            "fact_key": fact.key,
            "fact_value": fact.value,
            "source_memory_id": memory_id
        })
    # Layer 3 — rewrite CLAUDE.md
    await write_claude_md(project_id)

            1. All key technical decisions?

        )

## What aicli Is

**aicli is a shared AI memory platform for software development teams.**

The core problem it solves: every time you switch between Claude CLI, Cursor, the web UI, or any other AI tool, you have to re-explain your codebase, decisions, and context from scratch. aicli fixes this by giving every AI tool the same persistent project memory.

**Key capabilities:**

- **5-layer memory** — from in-session context all the way to global templates, synthesized automatically via Haiku after each session
- **Multi-LLM workflows** — visual pipeline designer (PM → Architect → Developer → Reviewer) with approval gates, running on LangGraph
- **Project knowledge graph** — every prompt, commit, and code change is tagged to features/bugs/tasks; searchable via pgvector semantic search
- **Role-based agents** — reusable agent personas (architect, reviewer, backend dev) composable into pipelines
- **Multi-user SaaS layer** — server-side API keys, billing, usage tracking, JWT auth
- **MCP server** — so Claude Code/Cursor can read the same project state natively

In short: it's an **AI memory layer + multi-agent workflow engine + project management UI**, all sharing one knowledge base.

---

| Competitor | Overlap |
|---|---|
| **mem0** | Closest competitor on the memory layer — persistent AI memory across tools/sessions |

Up. Here's the complete answer to the question:

---

## Why the work item pipeline wasn't using predefined roles

**Root cause (before today's fixes):** The original `work_item_pipeline.py` had all prompts hardcoded inline in the function — it never queried `mng_agent_roles`. It also always used the Anthropic API directly, hardcoded to Haiku, ignoring whatever was configured in the Roles tab.

**What was fixed in two passes today:**

**Pass 1** (earlier today): Added `_load_role(name)` helper that queries `mng_agent_roles` + joins system roles via `mng_role_system_links`. The function returns `(system_prompt, provider, model)`. The 4 stages now read from DB — PM uses "Product Manager" role, Architect uses "Sr. Architect", etc.

**Pass 2** (just now): The `_` discard on `provider` was the remaining bug — it loaded the right prompt but still routed all calls through `anthropic.AsyncAnthropic` directly, ignoring the role's `provider` field. For example, "Backend Developer" (provider=`deepseek`) would have silently called Anthropic with a DeepSeek model name and failed.

Now it dispatches correctly:
- `provider=claude` → `call_claude()` (async)
- `provider=deepseek` → `call_deepseek()` (async)
- `provider=openai` → `AsyncOpenAI.chat.completions.create()` (async)
- `provider=gemini` → `call_gemini()` via thread (async)
- `provider=grok` → `call_grok()` (async)

So if you configure the Developer stage to use "Backend Developer" (DeepSeek) or "Frontend Developer" (OpenAI GPT-4o) in the Roles tab, the pipeline will now actually call that provider.

output:
The `cli/` folder is the **new HTTP-only CLI client** — a terminal REPL that talks to the backend exclusively via HTTP, with no direct LLM provider code of its own.

**What it does:**
- Runs a `prompt_toolkit` interactive REPL (↑/↓ history, tab completion, Ctrl+C safe)
- Sends every user message to `POST /chat` on the backend
- Fetches `[Project Facts]` context from `GET /work-items/facts` before each prompt
- Loads `_system/aicli/context.md` as additional context injection
- Handles slash commands: `/memory`, `/switch <provider>`, `/project <name>`, `/history`, `/run <pipeline>`, `/status`
- Persists session state (project, provider, phase, feature) in `~/.aicli/cli_session.json`

**What it does NOT do (unlike the old `old/aicli/cli.py`):**
- No `anthropic`, `openai`, or any LLM SDK imports
- No local provider adapters or API key management
- No direct DB access
- No workflow execution logic

**How to run it:**
```bash
cd /path/to/aicli
python3.12 cli/cli.py
```

It requires the backend to be running at `http://localhost:8000` (configurable via `BACKEND_URL` env var or `~/.aicli/config.json`).

In short: it's a thin terminal front-end for the backend, the same way the Electron UI is a browser-based front-end. The old CLI (`old/aicli/cli.py`) was a standalone AI client with its own providers — the new one is just an HTTP client.

## Root cause

The Electron app (`old/ui/electron/main.js`) was launching the backend with:
```js
BACKEND_DIR = path.join(UI_ROOT, "backend")  // → old/ui/backend/
```
That old backend computed `workspace_dir = old/workspace` (doesn't exist) → every project lookup returned 404 or empty list.

## Fixes applied

1. **`old/ui/electron/main.js`** — changed `BACKEND_DIR` to use `ENGINE_ROOT` (= `aicli/` root) so it now starts from `aicli/backend/`
2. **`old/ui/start.sh`** — same path fix for manual starts

## To apply: restart aicli

**Quit the Electron app fully** then relaunch it (`npm start` from `old/ui/` or click the app icon). The Electron app will:
1. Kill whatever is on port 8000 (via `freePort()`)
2. Start the new backend from `aicli/backend/`
3. **Wait ~10–15 seconds** for the DB migration to complete on first launch — the app will show the project list once the backend signals ready

After that, `GET /projects/` returns `aicli` correctly and opening the project no longer throws 404.

These are not relative imports — they're **absolute imports that assume `backend/` is on `sys.path`**. They work when you `cd backend && uvicorn main:app` because Python adds the CWD to `sys.path`. The IDE just doesn't know that.

## Fix for both IDEs

**Option A — Simplest (PyCharm only)**

Right-click `backend/` folder → **Mark Directory as → Sources Root**. Done.

---

**Option B — Works everywhere (PyCharm + VS Code + pytest)**

Add `pyproject.toml` at `backend/`:

```toml
[tool.pytest.ini_options]
pythonpath = ["backend"]

[tool.pyright]
pythonPath = "backend"
```

Then configure the IDE run/debug target:

**VS Code** — create `.vscode/settings.json` at repo root:
```json
{
  "python.analysis.extraPaths": ["${workspaceFolder}/backend"],
  "python.envFile": "${workspaceFolder}/.env"
}
```

And `.vscode/launch.json`:
```json
{
  "configurations": [{
    "name": "Backend",
    "type": "python",
    "request": "launch",
    "module": "uvicorn",
    "args": ["main:app", "--host", "127.0.0.1", "--port", "8000"],
    "cwd": "${workspaceFolder}/backend",
    "env": { "PYTHONPATH": "${workspaceFolder}/backend" }
  }]
}
```

**PyCharm** — Edit Run Configuration → set **Working directory** to `backend/` and add **Environment variable** `PYTHONPATH=<absolute-path>/backend`.

---

**The short answer**: `PYTHONPATH=backend/` + working directory set to `backend/` is all you need. The imports stay as-is — no code changes required.

class ReactAgent:
    def __init__(
        self,
        role: str,
        tools: list,
        memory_store,
        max_steps: int = 15
    ):
        
    ## Sample  how to build prompt 
    def build_prompt(self, task: str, handoff: dict = None) -> list:
        system = SYSTEM_BASE + "\n\n" + ROLE_PROMPTS[self.role]
        
        user_content = f"Task: {task}"
        if handoff:
            user_content += f"\n\nHandoff from previous agent:\n{json.dumps(handoff, indent=2)}"
        
        return {
            "system": system,
            "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": user_content}]
        }

    async def run(self, task: str, handoff: dict = None) -> dict:
        prompt = self.build_prompt(task, handoff)
        history = prompt["messages"].copy()
        
        for step in range(self.max_steps):
            response = await self.call_llm(
                system=prompt["system"],
                messages=history
            )
            
            parsed = self.parse_response(response)
            
            # Final answer reached
            if parsed.get("final_output"):
                output = parsed["final_output"]
                await self.save_to_memory(task, output)
                return output
            
            # Hallucination guard — no thought before action
            if not parsed.get("thought"):
                history.append({
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": "You must write Thought: before any action. Try again."
                })
                continue
            
            # Execute tool
            if parsed.get("action"):
                observation = await self.execute_tool(
                    parsed["action"]["tool"],
                    parsed["action"]["args"]
                )
                
                # Log the step
                self.steps.append({
                    "thought": parsed["thought"],
                    "action": parsed["action"],
                    "observation": observation
                })
                
                # Feed back into history
                history.append({"role": "assistant", "content": response})
                history.append({
                    "role": "user",
                    "content": f"Observation: {observation}"
                })
            
            # Loop detection
            if self.detect_loop():
                return {"error": "loop_detected", "steps": self.steps}
        
        return {"error": "max_steps_reached", "steps": self.steps}

    def detect_loop(self) -> bool:
        if len(self.steps) < 3:
            return False
        last = self.steps[-3:]
        actions = [s["action"]["tool"] for s in last]
        return len(set(actions)) == 1  # same tool 3 times in a row

    async def save_to_memory(self, task: str, output: dict):
        await self.memory.add(
            content=json.dumps(output),
            project_id=self.project_id,
            tags=[self.role, "handoff", "react-output"],
            metadata={"task": task, "role": self.role}
        )

# aicli — AI-Powered Development Platform

**Stop re-explaining your project to every AI tool. aicli gives every LLM the same shared memory — so Claude CLI, Cursor, your web UI, and your agents all pick up exactly where you left off.**

---

## What Is aicli?

aicli is a development acceleration platform that wraps your entire project in a persistent AI brain. It connects to the tools you already use — Claude Code, Cursor, your IDE, your browser — and ensures every AI session starts with full context: your architecture decisions, open features, recent commits, and team conventions. No more copy-pasting context. No more re-explaining your stack.

The result: your AI works faster, makes fewer mistakes, and stays aligned with your project from day one to launch.

---

## 5-Layer Memory Architecture

Most AI tools forget everything the moment you close the tab. aicli doesn't.

| Layer | What It Stores | How It's Used |
|-------|---------------|---------------|
| **Immediate** | Live conversation messages | Real-time context within a session |
| **Working** | Active task, current feature, last commit | Picked up instantly on next session |
| **Project** | Architecture decisions, living PROJECT.md, coding conventions | Injected into every prompt automatically |
| **Historical** | Full interaction log — every prompt, every decision, every bug fix | Searchable with semantic AI search |
| **Global** | Shared role prompts, workflow templates, hook scripts | Reused across all your clients and projects |

Every layer feeds the next. Your AI doesn't just remember — it *understands* the full arc of your project.

---

## Smart Tagging & Project Management

Every AI interaction in aicli is connected to your project plan — not buried in a chat log.

- **Features, bugs, and tasks** live in a structured Planner with lifecycle status (idea → design → development → review → done)
- Every prompt, commit, and AI response is **tagged to the relevant feature or bug** automatically or in on

### 1. Is embedding only triggered by `/memory`?

**No** — there are 4 different triggers:

| Trigger | What gets embedded | When |
|---|---|---|
| `/memory` endpoint | New history entries (since last run), new commits, all role files | On demand / Stop hook |

| Every role file save | The saved role content | When you edit a role in the Roles tab |
| `/search/ingest` | History + roles (manual) | Admin endpoint |

The Stop hook calls `/memory` in background, so after every Claude Code session, new history entries and any new commits get embedded automatically — but with ~15 seconds of delay.

---

### 2. Do the 3 ingest methods create 3 rows each time?

**No — each creates a different number of rows depending on the content size and structure:**

**`ingest_history`** → **always 1 row per history entry**
- No smart chunking. Just: `"Q: {user_input}\nA: {output}"` as a single blob
- `source_type="history"`, `source_id=timestamp`
- `chunk_index=0`, `chunk_type="full"`

**`ingest_roles`** → **N rows per role file** (splits at H2/H3 headings)
- Uses `smart_chunk_markdown()` — splits on `##` sections, sub-splits at `###` if section > 3000 chars
- A role file with 4 H2 sections → 4 rows
- `source_type="role"`, `source_id=relative_path_of_the_file`

**`ingest_commit`** → **1 + N rows per commit** (1 summary + 1 per changed file)
- Runs `git show --format=%B\n---DIFF--- {hash}` to get commit message + full unified diff
- Uses `smart_chunk_diff()`:
  - **Chunk 0** (summary): commit message + list of all changed files
  - **Chunk 1..N** (file_diff): one chunk per file in the diff, with the actual `+/-` lines
- `source_type="commit"`, `source_id=commit_hash`
